name: Surrey Hills (Holmbury / Leith Hill)
notes: Classic singletrack and steep climbs ~1h from SW London.

detail.mountain-biking: Ride the natural singletrack off Holmbury and Pitch Hill — Barry Knows Best and Yoghurt Pots — plus the Summer Lightning descent on Leith Hill.
detail.trail-running: Run the Greensand Way over Leith Hill Tower, Surrey's highest point at 294m, through pine and bracken.

name: Swinley Forest
notes: Fast, sandy purpose-built MTB trails — drains well after rain.

detail.mountain-biking: Lap the blue and red Look Out trails — fast, bermy, sandy singletrack that rides well even after rain.
detail.trail-running: Sandy heath-and-pine tracks around The Look Out and Caesar's Camp.

name: Box Hill
notes: The Zig Zag Road climb; busy on weekends.

detail.road-cycling: Climb the Olympic Zig Zag Road — 2.5km of switchbacks at ~5% to the National Trust viewpoint.
detail.trail-running: Run the North Downs Way and the Stepping Stones loop across the chalk slopes.
detail.running: The stepping-stones circuit with a steep staircase finish for hill reps.

name: Richmond Park
notes: Closest spin / run loop; flat-ish, deer everywhere.

detail.road-cycling: Spin the ~11km Tamsin Trail perimeter or the road loop — flat-ish, traffic-calmed, deer everywhere.
detail.running: Loop the Tamsin Trail (~11.5km) or join the parkrun on rolling gravel paths.

name: Stanage Edge (Peak District)
notes: Iconic gritstone edge. Long drive; best as a full day.

detail.climbing: Gritstone trad and bouldering along 4km of edge — classics like Flying Buttress (HVD) and the Plantation boulders.
detail.trail-running: Run the edge-top path linking Stanage, Burbage and Higger Tor over gritstone moorland.
detail.mountain-biking: Rocky, technical bridleways — the Stanage causeway and the Cut Gate classic nearby.

name: Portland (Dorset)
notes: Sunny south-coast limestone sport climbing; sea cliffs.

detail.climbing: Sun-trap limestone sport on sea cliffs — hundreds of bolted routes from f4 to 8a around Cheyne Wears and the Cuttings.

name: Snowdonia (Llanberis)
notes: Big mountain days; weather changes fast. Long haul from London.

detail.climbing: Mountain trad on the Llanberis Pass crags — Dinas Cromlech's Cenotaph Corner (E1 5c) — plus slate-quarry sport.
detail.trail-running: Run the Llanberis Path or the Snowdon Horseshoe over Crib Goch on Wales' highest peak (1085m).
detail.mountain-biking: Raw mountain descents with the Antur Stiniog uplift park close by.

name: Lake District (Keswick)
notes: Fells and crags; a 5h+ drive — weekend-trip territory.

detail.climbing: Borrowdale volcanic trad — Shepherd's Crag's Little Chamonix (VD) and a lifetime of mountain routes.
detail.trail-running: Run the Catbells ridge or the Newlands Horseshoe on classic Lakeland fells.
detail.mountain-biking: Technical natural descents off Skiddaw plus the Whinlatter trail centre.

name: Chiltern Hills (Wendover)
notes: Rolling chalk hills and beech woods NW of London.

detail.road-cycling: Roll the chalk lanes over Whiteleaf and Kop Hill — a steep 700m wall famous from hillclimb races.
detail.trail-running: Run the Ridgeway and Chiltern Way through beech woods and chalk combes.
detail.mountain-biking: Chalky bridleway singletrack around Wendover Woods and Aston Hill.

name: Forest of Dean
notes: Flowy MTB trails and a relaxed cycle network.

detail.mountain-biking: Ride the flowy Verderers blue and the Freeminers red from Cannop, with a downhill uplift on weekends.
detail.trail-running: Run the Family Cycle Trail and forest tracks around Cannop Ponds.

name: Harrison's Rocks (Tunbridge Wells)
notes: Southern sandstone bouldering / top-roping; dries slowly.

detail.climbing: Southern sandstone top-roping and bouldering — Unclimbed Wall and Slim Finger Crack on soft, slow-drying rock.
detail.trail-running: Run the High Weald woodland trails linking Harrison's and Eridge Rocks.

name: South Downs (Devil's Dyke)
notes: Open chalk downland with sea views above Brighton.

detail.road-cycling: Climb the Devil's Dyke road out of Brighton and ride the quiet chalk-downland lanes.
detail.trail-running: Run the South Downs Way along the escarpment with sea views to Brighton.
detail.running: Open downland loops around Britain's largest dry valley.

name: Bedgebury Forest (Kent)
notes: Forestry England playground of pine and lakes with a proper graded MTB loop.

detail.mountain-biking: Ride the 13km red Singletrack Trail with its berms, drops and rock features, or warm up on the 9km blue Family Cycle Trail.
detail.trail-running: Loop the waymarked walking trails around Marshal's Lake on rolling pine-needle singletrack.
detail.running: Easy traffic-free laps on the firm gravel family routes through the National Pinetum.

name: Ashdown Forest (East Sussex)
notes: High Weald heathland with big skies and one savage little road climb.

detail.trail-running: Run the Gill's Lap circular and the open heath linking the Wealdway and Vanguard Way over 223m Camp Hill.
detail.running: Loop the open-access heathland from Gill's Lap on sandy, springy tracks with long views.
detail.road-cycling: Take on Kidd's Hill, 'The Wall' — 1.5km at 8% (16% max), centrepiece of the Hell of the Ashdown sportive.

name: Friston Forest (East Sussex)
notes: Beech woodland tucked behind the Seven Sisters cliffs and the Cuckmere meander.

detail.trail-running: String the forest tracks into the South Downs Way and the Seven Sisters clifftop for a coast-and-woods loop.
detail.running: Sheltered woodland laps from Butchershole, out of the wind off the chalk cliffs.
detail.mountain-biking: Ride the 9km red Jeremy Cole MTB Trail from Butchershole into fast beech singletrack.

name: Queen Elizabeth Country Park (Hampshire)
notes: Steep chalk-and-beech hangers on the South Downs Way with proper graded MTB.

detail.mountain-biking: Hit the 7.5km red trail with the bermed 'Snakebite' section, or the flowier 6km blue.
detail.trail-running: Climb Butser Hill and run the South Downs Way ridge straight out of the visitor centre.
detail.running: Steep woodland loops up War Down and Holt Down for a lung-busting hill session.
detail.road-cycling: A base for the quiet Meon Valley lanes and South Downs gravel links.

name: Mam Tor & Hope Valley (Peak District)
notes: The Great Ridge skyline above Castleton plus one of Britain's most brutal road passes.

detail.trail-running: Run the Great Ridge from Mam Tor over Hollins Cross and Back Tor to Lose Hill on a flagged ridgeline.
detail.running: The ~11km Mam Tor circular from Castleton with a stiff climb to the 517m summit.
detail.road-cycling: Grind up Winnats Pass — a Cat 3 wall averaging ~10% with 20% ramps out of Castleton.

name: Cleeve Hill & the Cotswolds (Gloucestershire)
notes: The roof of the Cotswolds — golden-stone escarpment, classic climbs and the Cotswold Way.

detail.road-cycling: Climb Cleeve Hill (2.4km at 6%) and link Leckhampton and Birdlip Hills for an escarpment loop.
detail.running: Loop Cleeve Common, the Cotswolds' highest point at 330m, on open grassland tracks.
detail.trail-running: Run the Cotswold Way National Trail along the escarpment edge with valley-wide views.

name: Symonds Yat & Wye Valley (Herefordshire)
notes: Limestone crag above a wooded loop of the River Wye (partial closure since the 2023 rockfall).

detail.climbing: Trad limestone up to E-grades — classics like Red Rose Speedway (HVS 5a) and Whitt (VS 4c) on the reopened southern buttresses.
detail.road-cycling: Spin the riverside Wye Valley lanes and take on the short, steep Symonds Yat climb (~7% avg, 20% max).

name: Cheddar Gorge (Mendips, Somerset)
notes: Britain's deepest gorge — soaring limestone walls and a top-100 road climb in one place.

detail.climbing: Long limestone trad and sport: the 5-pitch Coronation Street (E1 5b) or routes from f4 to 8c (south side open ~Oct–Mar).
detail.road-cycling: Ride the iconic Cheddar Gorge climb — 4.2km of hairpins and 16% ramps, #1 in Britain's 100 Greatest Cycling Climbs.

name: Avon Gorge (Bristol)
notes: Bold, polished limestone right under the Clifton Suspension Bridge in the heart of the city.

detail.climbing: Technical, run-out trad — Main Wall classics like Great Central Route and Malbogies (both HVS 5a), or the easy multi-pitch Morpheus (VD 4b) at Sea Walls.

name: Swanage & Purbeck (Dorset)
notes: Jurassic limestone sea cliffs and ledges — the spiritual home of UK deep-water soloing.

detail.climbing: Sea-cliff trad in the Boulder Ruckle (Finale Groove, HVS 4c), bolted sport at Dancing Ledge, and DWS off Conner Cove.
detail.trail-running: Run the South West Coast Path from Swanage to Old Harry Rocks and around Durlston Head.
detail.running: Clifftop and downland loops above Durlston with the sea on one side the whole way.

name: Dartmoor (Haytor, Devon)
notes: Wild granite moor of weathered tors, bog and big skies for a proper weekend escape.

detail.climbing: Granite tor routes and bouldering — Raven's Gully (Severe 4b) on Haytor and Bonehill Rocks problems from V4 upward.
detail.trail-running: Run the open moor between the tors — the Ten Tors and Two Moors Way lines cross classic wild terrain.
detail.running: Loop Haytor and Hound Tor on grassy moorland tracks with granite underfoot.

name: Exmoor (Lynton & Porlock, Devon/Somerset)
notes: Coastal moorland plunging to the sea, home to the UK's steepest A-road.

detail.road-cycling: Climb Porlock Hill, Britain's steepest A-road at up to 1-in-4, then haul up to Dunkery Beacon (519m).
detail.trail-running: Run the South West Coast Path through the Valley of Rocks and the Two Moors Way out of Lynmouth.
detail.running: Moor-and-coast loops above Lynton on grassy clifftop and combe paths.

name: Brecon Beacons (Pen y Fan, Powys)
notes: South Wales' highest peaks — sweeping grassy ridges made for big mountain days.

detail.trail-running: Run the Fan Dance from Storey Arms over Corn Du and Pen y Fan (886m), extending to Cribyn and Fan y Big.
detail.running: The Pen y Fan and Corn Du horseshoe — a runnable summit circuit on engineered mountain paths.
detail.road-cycling: Climb the A470 pass over Storey Arms and explore the quiet valley roads around Talybont.

name: Afan Forest Park (Glyncorrwg, South Wales)
notes: Steep, forested valleys laced with some of the best purpose-built singletrack in Britain.

detail.mountain-biking: Ride the 24km red Y Wâl with its famous Zig Zags descent, the flowing Penhydd, or the 44km black W2 epic.
detail.trail-running: Follow the forest valley trails and old tramways climbing high above Glyncorrwg.

name: BikePark Wales (Merthyr Tydfil)
notes: The UK's biggest uplift-served gravity park — 50+ graded trails and a van to the top.

detail.mountain-biking: Take the uplift and pick your line — the UK's longest blue descent Terry's Belly, fast natural Vicious Valley (red), or expert Dai Hard (black).

name: Coed y Brenin (Snowdonia, Gwynedd)
notes: The UK's original purpose-built trail centre, deep in the forests of southern Eryri.

detail.mountain-biking: Ride the flowy blue MinorTaur, the technical red Temtiwr and Cyflym Coch, or commit to the 38km black Beast of Brenin.
detail.trail-running: Dedicated waymarked trail-running routes wind through the forest alongside the bike trails.
